content:
{
  "title": "Counterfactual-Contrastive Prompting for Factuality in LLMs",
  "problem": "Large language models often hallucinate non-factual content, especially when given ambiguous or open-ended prompts. Existing prompting strategies insufficiently constrain models to distinguish between plausible but false statements and verified facts.",
  "motivation": "Improving LLM factuality is critical for trustworthy downstream applications. While retrieval-augmented generation and instruction tuning have made progress, hallucinations persist due to models' tendencies to overgeneralize from learned patterns. A prompting method that explicitly forces LLMs to contrast factual and counterfactual information could sharpen their discrimination.",
  "method": "We propose Counterfactual-Contrastive Prompting, where each factual prompt is paired with a minimally-edited counterfactual prompt (e.g., 'What year did the Apollo 11 land on the Moon?' vs. 'What year did Apollo 12 land on Mars?'). Prompts are grouped and LLMs are conditioned to identify which statements are factual and which are not, either via explicit labels or by ranking. This contrastive schema exploits the model's ability to differentiate subtle factual shifts and discourages plausible hallucinations by requiring explicit discrimination between closely-related truths and falsehoods. The framework can be layered atop existing instruction or retrieval-based prompts.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate on fact-checking benchmarks such as FEVER and TriviaQA, using standard and hallucination-prone prompts. Baselines include vanilla prompting, Chain-of-Thought, and retrieval-augmented generation. Metrics will be factual accuracy, hallucination rate, and discriminative confidence. Human evaluation will assess real/fake distinction in outputs."
}
